comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Ross Nichols, founder of the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids (OBOD) and, alongside Lewis Spence, one of the two most influential 20th century authors on Druidry in the English-speaking world. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a few questions: Is the mental plane eternal like the spiritual plane?

I've seen some writings talking about demons coming from "below" the material plane. What does that mean?

Finally, are there ways to know details about future lives?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's the usual teaching, as mental plane phenomena like the laws of logic and mathematics don't seem to change over time.

2) The idea here is that the planes we usually talk about -- spiritual, mental, astral, etheric, material -- are the ones that human beings can experience. There are planes above the spiritual plane which are out of our reach, and planes below the material plane likewise.

3) No, because they haven't happened yet. What you do right now, and in the rest of this life, will have a powerful influence on what your future lives will be like.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If human beings exist on the mental and spiritual planes, which are eternal, then shouldn't it be possible to get knowledge of the future from the parts of us that are eternal?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure, when you reach the point that you can perceive those planes directly. As a human being, however, your consciousness is focused on the material plane, and it will continue to focus on that plane until you've finished your time in material incarnation. After that, you get to do the same work on the etheric plane, and so on. Some millions of years from now, you'll be ready to leave time behind and focus your consciousness on the mental plane; until then, I wouldn't worry about it too much. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Your answer to question 2 seems to imply though that not everything starts on the spiritual plane and works its way down. Isn't that usually the occult teaching though?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: From an other-than-human perspective, all things pass through the spiritual plane from planes further up, and then descend through the planes from there, eventually passing below the material plane to affect things further down. Since we're human, it makes sense for us to think of things as starting from the spiritual plane, since that's the highest plane we can (very dimly) perceive.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: But this teaching would seem to contradict the idea that demons come from below. Is there a way to reconcile these ideas that's commonly used in the occult community?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, it doesn't contradict that at all. Demons can interact with us only when we descend to their level. Certain events early in the process of human evolution opened the possibility of contact between our plane and theirs, and now and then -- deliberately, in the case of certain kinds of magic, or accidentally, in a variety of ways -- a channel gets opened and human beings come into contact with them.

There's a great scene in Christopher Marlowe's play Doctor Faustus where Faustus is talking with Mephistopheles, and the doctor asks the demon how it is that he's able to get out of Hell and talk to humans. Mephistopheles' answer is classic: "Why, this is Hell, nor am I ever out of it." That's the secret of demonic evocation. It's the easiest thing in the world: all you have to do is debase your consciousness to their level, and there they are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You said in Monsters that the link with humans occurs at the astral level. Where can I read more about this subject?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I wrote Monsters in 1999 and 2000. I've learned a few things since then.

As for where you can read more about such things, I suggest a course of reading in classic occult philosophy, beginning with Levi's Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What kind of events would accidentally open a channel?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sufficiently careless mucking around with Ouija boards or other pop-culture occult gimmicks; anything that even roughly approximates blood sacrifice, outside of a well-understood religious context (such as Santeria) where that's a normal part of worship; or prolonged wallowing in selfish and destructive emotional states, when there's a past life connection with them, or a local site that's been used for malefic magic or demonolatry to provide them with a portal.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What sort of event in human evolution are you talking about, and how early?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There are various accounts of it in the magical literature, which disagree about the details.

comments.title: Cunning in a bottle
comments.content:
JMG,
Not a question, but uncomfortably close to witnessing:
Some years ago you had put the idea across my bow of looking into learning about curanderismo due to being located in the American southwest. This past summer I did a comparative study between curanderismo and English cunning work. As it seems to me that curanderismo is very family, ethnic, and neighborhood-oriented I don’t see much of a future in trying to force my way into a culture which I have a fondness and respect for, but am nevertheless an outsider to.

For a simple beginning cunning-work I decided upon a witch bottle/charm against the evil eye. Something every house should have as a matter of course, yah?

Feeling that the old ways are often the best ways the bottle mixture was urine and 9 bent pins, with 3 extemporized incantations over each pin as it was placed into the bottle. The bottle was corked and hidden at the northernmost point of the house, which is also the northernmost point of my workshop.
One week later I came home to find my brother pacing nervously in and out of the house, glaring daggers at everyone in the house, cursing at the empty air, and behaving in a generally terrifying manner. He had been living rent-free in the back room of the house for the last year after the meltdown of the latest in a long line of failed relationships and lost career opportunities (he is an extremely talented musician). On confronting him about his alarming behavior he began ranting about sexual jealousies imagined, phantom plots directed against him, and rapidly descended into completely irrational accusations.

The evening ended two hours later with my mother-in-law, wife, and daughter barricaded in the back rooms, and my brother transported to the ER and then to a jail cell for domestic violence charges. After the incident we found the fuel for the evening’s events: an empty sixpack and a liter bottle of tequila half empty.

Everyone in the house was blindsided by the entire incident because it seemed to have come out of nowhere. Then I remembered the working.
On the night of the working, Sunday, October 14th, the moon was a waxing crescent, 30% visible.
A week later, the night of the incident the moon was waxing gibbous, 95% visible sliding into full after midnight.

I really only made the connection between the working and the incident upon reflection over the next two days as I had let the working fade into the background of my memory, and because the original intent of the working had been to repel the malicious eye from forces outside the house not inside.
And then the final piece fell into place. I eventually realized that the bottle used in the working was a mini antique tequila bottle.

Magic works, but not always the way we expect it to.

.:E:.

replies.title: Re: Cunning in a bottle
replies.content: Yes, it does. If your brother was the principal source of negative energy in the household, your spell will have banished him -- as indeed it did. I hope nobody got hurt.

replies.title: Re: Cunning in a bottle
replies.content: Thankfully, no one innocent was hurt. I am simultaneously listening to your first appearance on Niles Heckman's podcast just now. In particular I caught your mention of the Knapp Hall deck. Googling the deck images what stands out immediately is that the fool and the magus wear the same outfit in contrast to two entirely different outfits as shown in the Rider-Waite deck. Definitely feeling that ragged existence these days and wondering what the next adventure of wise folly is coming my way.

comments.title: illness and magical practice
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
My health is improving with the solar and telluric current exercises and benefiting from my Druid practice in general. That said, i still do get ill and sometimes overtired. I try to do my meditation and ritual workings early in the day to compensate for that.

What should one do during an acute illness such as a bad case of the flu? Is it similar to intoxication?

Maxine

replies.title: Re: illness and magical practice
replies.content: Pretty much, yes. It's been a while since I've gotten sick enough to need to skip my practices, but that's the best thing to do if you're too ill to go about the ordinary activities of life. Say a prayer or two to your deity of choice, and leave it at that.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I'm reading some Buddhist philosophy, and keep coming across the Buddha's parinirvana, and it means "blowing out", and when the Buddha obtained it, he stopped being reincarnated. This raises a question: is it possible for a soul to will itself out of existence?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sorry to butt in, but it really depends upon what you mean by existence.  The Buddha stopped being reborn, which is different in Buddhist thinking from non-existence.  In fact, in many Buddhist traditions it is believed that he will return as the Maitreya Buddha, but not until the teachings of the last Buddha are no longer practiced.  Parinirvana just means nirvana, or awakened state, after death, and it is achieved by expiating the karma that leads to rebirth.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It is entirely possible my question is based on a misunderstanding of the philosophy. I will freely admit I found it odd, and it seems distinctly possible I just misunderstood what it meant.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hmm. The Buddhist teachings I was exposed to in my youth, by way of a Japanese stepfamily who are Shingon Buddhists, had it that Maitreya is a bodhisattva right now; the historical Buddha Shakyamuni has passed into nirvana and will not return. I'm fascinated to hear that in other traditions of Buddhism Shakyamuni will return as Maitreya; that seems very strange in the light of Buddhist tradition generally.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That sounds like the Amitabha Buddha, or the Buddha of the West, who plays a large part in Shingon Buddhism.  He is one of 13 bodhisattvas whom the Shingon traditionally venerate.  My understanding is that, at the moment, Maitreya is hanging out in Tusita heaven, waiting to strut and fret his hour upon the stage of the human realm.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's what I was taught as well -- Tusita Heaven is where the highest-ranking bodhisattva in each cycle braces himself for his plunge into human incarnation as a Buddha. Amitabha, though, is a different kettle of fish -- as I recall, he's not a bodhisattva at all, but a Buddha; he went through his stint in Tusita Heaven and his time on earth proclaiming the Dharma countless cycles of time in the past.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In Druid teachings, it's possible to mess up badly enough that you tumble all the way back to the Cauldron of Annwn, the storehouse of raw spiritual substance not yet formed into individual souls, and dissolve into the mass; such a soul ceases to exist as an individual being, though its raw "soul-stuff" gets recycled, so to speak. I suppose you could do that deliberately if you really, really wanted to, but it seems like an immense waste of effort.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
When doing the Spirit Above and Below receptive workings from the DMH, I originally coordinated my breath with the energy flow direction--energy into fingertips on inbreath, and out of fingertips into midline on outbreath.

Then I realized that the instructions say to spend *minutes* on both of those steps, and of course I cannot breathe that slowly. So I tried to disconnect my breath from the energy flow direction, but have not been able to do so completely.

For example, I can intake energy on the inbreath, and then intake more energy on the next inbreath. Or I can intake energy on the inbreath and then just as the outbreath is about to reverse the energy flow, decide that I will intake energy on the outbreath too--but each time I breathe in or out I have to recalibrate. (I hope I am describing this intelligibly.)

Is it alright to have the energy flow coordinated with breathing? Do you have a reccommended way to do this? Or would it be best to disconnect them?

(Thank you for Magic Mondays!)

replies.title: Breathing in energy
replies.content:
Hi,
Thanks for asking because I do that too also with Solar and Telluric current work. It just seems natural. With the Solar and telluric currents, I release the energy into my body on the exhale. I bet many of us are doing this so it is good you asked!
Maxine

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In the energy-collecting phase, I tend to draw in energy with each inbreath and let it pool and establish itself in my hands on the outbreath. In the charging-the-midline phase, I let the energy flow out continuously irrespective of the breath. Still, there are other ways to do this, and your best bet is to experiment until you find the way that works best for you.

comments.title: Rose Cross-style rituals
comments.content:
Hello

(1) What are Rose Cross-style rituals?

(2) And what are they for?

Regards

replies.title: Re: Rose Cross-style rituals
replies.content:
1) You can read instructions for the Rose Cross ritual here. There are several other rituals of the same kind keyed to different magical systems -- for example, in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn, the OIW Ritual corresponds to the Rose Cross ritual.

2) Here's what the Golden Dawn lecture on the subject says:

"1. It encloses the aura with a protection against outside influences. It is like a veil. The Pentagrams protect, but they also light up the astral and make entities aware of you. They are more positive for magical working. When much distracted, use the Pentagrams to banish and the Rose-cross to maintain peace.

"2. It is a call to another mode of your consciousness and withdraws you from the physical. It is a good preparation for meditation and, combined with the Key-Word, a form of invocation of the Higher Wisdom which is helpful when solving problems or preparing for a difficult interview, or in order to be calm and strong to help another.

"3. When you are quite familiar with the Ritual, but most certainly not before, it can be done in imagination while resting or lying down. Part of yourself goes out, and you get all the sensation of walking around your own quiescent body. Used thus, with rhythmic breathing, it will withdraw your mind from pain (if it be not too severe) and release you for sleep. You can do the analysis of the Key-Word standing behind your physical head, and you can call down the Divine White Brilliance, watching it flow over your body and smooth out the tangles in the etheric double, bringing peace and rest.

"4. You can do the Ritual with intention to help others in pain or difficulty. For this purpose, you build up an astral image of the person, in the centre of the room, and call down the Light upon him, after surrounding him with the six crosses. When the ceremony is done, command the astral shape you have made to return to the person, bearing with it the peace of Yeheshuah.

"5. It is a protection against psychic invasion from the thoughts of others or from disturbed psychic conditions, such as there might be in a place charged with fear, where terrible things had happened."

replies.title: Re: Rose Cross-style rituals
replies.content:
"When the ceremony is done, command the astral shape you have made to return to the person, bearing with it the peace of Yeheshuah."

"Return"? So a piece is borrowed for that?

replies.title: Re: Rose Cross-style rituals
replies.content: No, all that's borrowed is the image of the person, and that's what returns.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've been leaving vinegar out at night for a few weeks now. I found you don't need much of it. Just pouring a bit onto a plate and swirling it round so the flat of the plate is wet is sufficient. As long as a bit is still liquid in the morning, you had all night protection. If you want to enhance the effect or need it in a room in a hurry, you can open the bottle and blow into it, which spreads the vapour around quickly.

Within a day or two of starting, my digestion improved. That was very welcome, although the implication that a ghost or malicious wizard has been interfering with my bowels is...unsettling. :)

Apart from if you're trying to have an out of body experience, is there any other time you shouldn't use the vinegar?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Any time you're doing magical workings of a kind that requires you to condense etheric energy into a dense, nearly physical form, the vinegar gets in the way. Thus if you're practicing Franz Bardon's elemental workings, for example, do it in an unvinegared room! Other than that, you should be fine.

Wow! Cool question. I'd say an "Evil Dead" kind of question.
I'm eager to read JMG's answer...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have no idea. I don't mess around with dead things.

Eager man here,

Kind of attenuating or even cutting off energetic connections or cleaning it from miasmas if you will, which could be carried on to the next life.

comments.title: Race and spiiritual/magical practices
comments.content:
What are your thoughts on certain spiritual or magical practices being suitable for specific races and less suitable for others?

It seems to be a common idea in older books on magic and spirituality. Of course, the modern idea is that everybody back then was a misguided racist and there can be no discussion on this matter, but I do wonder if there is not some nuance that we have lost in our desire to be seen as much more progressive and tolerant than anyone before us.

Thanks again for providing this forum for questions,

B

replies.title: Re: Race and spiiritual/magical practices
replies.content: I think, whether biology matters, culture would play a huge role, which is also "racist" these days....

replies.title: Re: Race and spiiritual/magical practices
replies.content: As far as I can tell, the differences are minor if they exist at all. I know people of African and Asian descent who are first-rate Golden Dawn ceremonial magicians, people whose ancestors came from Ireland and Germany who find African-diaspora religious such as Vodoun and Santeria profoundly satisfying, and successful practitioners of yoga and esoteric Buddhism of every skin color and ethnic origin you care to think of. That being the case, I think that in this case, the pursuit of tolerance has something going for it.

comments.title: Meditation, Essenes, Bath and Caffeine
comments.content:
Thank you for Magic Mondays.

1. I have been doing the relaxation meditation described in DH in the mornings. I am the poster a couple of weeks ago who felt uncomfortable calling upon deities and asked if I could just work with my guardian angel. You said to ask my angel. If I'm not hearing a clear answer on this, I'm assuming it's because I'm such a beginner at meditation, and I just need to continue?

2. I have begun the New Thought Order of Essenes newsletters you linked to. Is that something where I should focus on it completely until I finish it? Doing that for six months might be a good way to put off my deity issue with DH, but still feel like I'm working toward more spiritual practice and comfort. I'll be six months into my morning meditation by that time too.

3. I am wanting to do a hoodoo bath, but when I went to the park nearby and mentally explained the bath and asked if I could dump the water by a pair of robust trees, I didn't hear anything, even after a long time. I'm assuming this too is because I'm too much of a beginner at meditation. But dumping the water on them anyways is kind of rude, right?

4. I have been meditating in the morning, before my first cup of joe, based on the recommendation in DH to not consume caffeine. I am assuming you don't drink coffee or espresso. Do you recommend herbal tea only, so no black or green tea?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Meditation, Essenes, Bath and Caffeine
replies.content:
1) Relax. You don't need to hear the answer consciously. Just keep practicing and let the results show up when it's time for them to show up.

2) No, one of the interesting things about New Thought is that it blends very well with most other kinds of esotericism. You can work with the Order of Essenes material while you do the DH work.

3) You may not perceive the answer in terms of hearing. Did you get a feeling one way or the other?

4) The point is simply not to take in caffeine when you're trying to calm yourself for meditation; you can certainly have a cup of coffee afterwards. I don't drink coffee because it gives me migraines, but I drink a lot of tea -- just not right before my morning meditation.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Based on your recommendation I have commissioned a natal reading from Christopher Warnock. I don't know much about astrology, so was wondering what kind of questions would be pertinent to ask him as followup when I receive it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That depends entirely on what's going on in your life that you might want to know about!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings,

Long(ish)-time lurker here, breaking my silence for a moment to ask something that may have an obvious answer, but I just wanted to double-check...

You’ve mentioned before that sitting cross-legged makes something like a closed energy circuit in the body, thus preventing energy from coming up from the Earth. This has gotten me thinking - I use casting sticks for my Geomancy divinings, and as part of the casting, I try to envision a connection between the sticks and the Earth (through me as a conduit of sorts) while I focus on the query. Since I do it this way, would sitting cross-legged in any way impede my ability to perform a successful casting? Perhaps not preventing it entirely, but at the very least making it more difficult?
I'm still a beginner and the last thing I want to do is make things harder for myself than I need to, heh...

-Shadow

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nah, that shouldn't be a problem at all. This thing we call "energy" includes many different levels and modes of activity. Sitting cross-legged affects energy exchange on the etheric plane, while divination mostly involves the astral plane, with some input from the mental plane; it's a little like worrying whether closing the blinds will keep out radio waves.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ah, okay! That is good to know, thank you very much!

-S

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello and happy Monday,

Have you listened to Rune Soup's latest episode with Mitch Horowitz? They talk a lot about New Thought, it's an interesting discussion.

Hope you have a good day.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, I don't have time to listen to a lot of podcasts. Glad to hear that Mitch is out there getting some publicity for his new book!

replies.title: I listened
replies.content: I listened to it, and found it rather interesting.  I also went back and found Horowitz's previous one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John—

What are the differences, if any, between a sorcerer, mage, priest, and shaman? I’ve seen these terms used in many contexts, some interchangeably, and I’ve often wondered if there were formal distinctions.

David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. They're just words, and like most words, they're used in contested and confusing ways by different people. Your dictionary will be as useful here as anything else.

comments.title: Number meditation
comments.content:
Good morning John Michael,
I've gotten stuck on the numbers exercise in lesson six of the LRM where we are to meditate on 1-10. I've found an article by WW Westcott about Pythagorean mathematics that describes some useful perspective but I seem to be especially thick re the subject. I am wondering if there are other authors or sources you would recommend to help improve my understanding.
Many thanks.

replies.title: Re: Number meditation
replies.content: Don't worry about it. In this work you're not required to get the right answer; in this work, very often, there is no right answer. The goal is to wrestle with the properties of number and see if those provide you with a useful vocabulary of symbols. If they don't, leave them for future reference.

replies.title: Re: Number meditation
replies.content: Thank you, your answer helps with a host of difficulties!

comments.title: Temple of the Ovate Grade
comments.content:
Dear John,

after setting the temple of the ovate grade for the first time and performing a LRP in that space, Should every daily performance of the ritual afterwards be done in the temple or is the altar to be dismantled until further needed down the lessons? I'm thinking here of the logistics of having to use the same room for different activities at different times, and also of the consequences of burning incense daily in the same room. In connection with the latest, I take when you speak of incense it's frankincense you're referring to?
As usual, many , many thanks, I'm enjoying the work through the Ovate grade (slowly!) and just got through the post your encyclopedia of the occult, looks superb!
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Temple of the Ovate Grade
replies.content: I take down the temple and put everything away as soon as the ritual's over with, as I don't have a spare room to devote purely to magical practices. (I do my magical work in the same study where I write.) As for incense, you can use any kind of incense you like; I personally prefer Japanese and Korean incenses, as they have a lighter smoke that yields less dust, and the scents also conduce to meditation.

replies.title: Re: Temple of the Ovate Grade
replies.content:
Many thanks John, just to clarify: Should the temple then be assembled every day before performing the LRP, or can we keep practicing the LRP without the temple structure? Incense-wise, does the choice apply as well to cones and sticks? (using a stick holder instead of a sand-filled censer/bowl)
Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: Temple of the Ovate Grade
replies.content: I only assemble a temple when I'm going to be doing the temple opening and closing ritual. The rest of the time, I just do the LRP. Yes, you can use cones if you like, or loose incense on charcoal.

comments.title: Egregores
comments.content: Dear JMG. A question, perhaps whimsical, perhaps not. But I have been wondering: would a political 'movement' have an egregore? If so, what would would the egregore of the SJW left look like right now? Angry? Splintered? United? Out of control? There is obviously a huge, onward-rushing energy there, but it seems fuelled by righteous anger, and not healthily. How might we understand this in esoteric terms?

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. Any group of people united by common passions and a common goal forms a group mind, and when a group mind exists for a prolonged period and is regularly reinforced by emotional energy, it gives rise to an egregor. One of the major difficulties in politics is that egregors feed back the emotions that created them into the people who participate in them, and if those emotions are toxic enough, you can get a really impressive crash dive into dysfunction, violence, and madness.

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: I wonder now: would (for example) the Democrats and Republicans have two distinct egregors, or would the egregors be intermingled to some extent due to belonging to the same country?

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: They have different egregors. The people who belong to each party also participate in the egregor of the United States, which is a different thing.

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: Would such an egregor be limited to a single country? If, to use one example, lots of Canadians identify with Democrats, could they feed into and be influenced by the Democratic egregor?

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: Egregors don't worry about political boundaries. If a group of Canadians identify with the Democratic party and feed its egregor with their emotional energy, they're going to be influenced by it.

Several of us came from reading your current Ecosophia blog. We raised a glass to JMG and other absent friends!

best
Phil H

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

Many thanks
Phil H

comments.title: LBRP
comments.content: Any suggestions for Welsh goddesses that have the requisite four letters? I've found a handful of gods-Bran, Math, Lleu, Beli-but not goddesses. Would it be more beneficial to pick two goddesses and never mind the number of letters in the name rather than go with four gods? Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: LBRP
replies.content: Well, Elen of the Roads is one easy option! The other name that's commonly used among Welsh Druids is Sulw -- this is an old Welsh form of Sul, the Celtic name of the goddess of Aquae Sulis aka Bath. She's a goddess of healing and of the hidden sun beneath the earth, and is a daughter of Beli.

comments.title: Alphabets
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

Are you aware of any attempts to use the Bobileth and Beth-luis-non alphabets in magic, or know of any arguments against doing so?

I encountered them both in “The Magical Arts of Celtic Britian” by Lewis Spence. The Beth-luis-non he shows an image of is nothing like Ogham.

My searches online turned up nothing.

Yours in Druidry,

Brigyn

ps: If I may be so bold, a second question: I see you reference Franz Bardon in your book about the Coelbren. Have you considered that the Coelbren might be an excellent candidate for a system of Quabbalistic speech?

replies.title: Re: Alphabets
replies.content:
Now there's a blast from the past! I'm pretty sure that the circle of Scottish Druids that gathered around Spence used both of them, and so did some other early to mid-20th century Druid groups. Your challenge will be working out the symbolic meanings of the letters.

PS. It's already in process. Bardon's one helpful reference for comparative purposes; another is kotodama, the Japanese system of word-magic.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: From what I can tell, the mind training of occultism, New Thought and stoicism all involve a degree of alienation from your own emotions. You either control your emotions or distance yourself from them. Is this accurate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, that's putting the cart before the horse. You don't distance yourself from your emotions, you recognize that the distance has always existed -- that you are not your emotions, and that when emotions drown your capacity to think or sense or act effectively, it's because they're running amuck. Nor do you need to control your emotions -- you just control how you react to them. I can get as angry, or as lustful, or as grumpy as the next guy; I just know that these are things I'm feeling, objects of experience in the same way that a bird flying past my window is an object of experience, and decide on the basis of a broader range of things how I want to respond to them.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Due to my mistake several animals that I was in charge of unnecessary suffered before they died. Can you recommend a ritual that can help me to let them know that I'm very sorry and ask for forgiveness?

I don't practice any magical system regularly but I've studied The Druidry Handbook and generally feel drawn mostly to Druidic and Northern European polytheism ideas and practices.

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: One of the standard things to do in Western occultism is to make an offering to their spirits in the form of an appropriate donation -- for example, to a charity that works to prevent cruelty to animals, or one that rehabilitates animals that would otherwise be killed. Make it large enough that you have to give up some casual expenses in order to afford it. When you are making the donation, speak to the spirits of the animals you harmed, and ask for their forgiveness.

comments.title: Some clarification
comments.content:
I remember that in one of your posts (here or over at the "big" Ecosophia or maybe even back in the Archdruid Days), that the first part of the 20th century were the most robust of years for magic in "Black" America. I have been casting about for some reading or articles on this subject.

Brother, can you spare some knowledge?

All the best, and thanks in advance.

John
https://mightaswellliebackandenjoyit.blogspot.com/

replies.title: Re: Some clarification
replies.content: The book you want is Spiritual Merchants by Carolyn Morrow Long, a fine history of African-American folk religion and its evolution into the "spiritual products" business. Enjoy!

comments.title: Karma
comments.content: What, if any, are some recommended divinatory practices that would help give insights into karmatic connections between friends and couples?

replies.title: Re: Karma
replies.content: That depends entirely on what kind of divination you, personally, are good at. Some people can read karmic connections very clearly in Tarot; others can't. The same is true of astrology or, well, any other oracle you care to name. The personal equation is the one that matters most.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Just a word of thanks...I'm undergoing radiation therapy for a recurrent cancer. My first week was pretty awful, with significant complications. Then I remembered your TSW™ and 800-lb. gorilla posts and enlisted my Catholic heritage. I had the therapists turn off the radio and started saying the Rosary during my therapy. Within two days my symptoms had cleared up completely and now I'm sailing through as if I was visiting a spa, not getting bombarded by high-energy radiation. TSW, indeed!

RPC

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm delighted to hear it. May you make a complete and painless recovery!

comments.title: Four-fold breathing: FILO or FIFO
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

A couple of days ago, I was flipping through a book on Yoga, and noticed that the instructions for the 'Four-fold breathing' exercise given there were quite different from the ones I've found in your books The Druidry Handbook and The Celtic Golden Dawn.

Inhaling is the same: starting from the bottom of the lungs, you depress the diaphragm (although it recommends not to relax the abdomen and allow it to expand, but to control it gently using the abs), then proceed to the midriff and finally the top of the lungs. Exhaling, however, is exactly the reverse of what you advise: starting emptying the lungs from the top, then lowering the ribcage the midriff, and finally using the diaphragm to wring out the remaining air. The book uses the metaphor of a glass, which you fill from the bottom, but empty from the top.

To use a bit of IT jargon, the two techniques are FILO (first-in, last-out) vs FIFO (first-in, first-out).

Since according to what you have said in various places Prana and Nwyfre are effectively the same energy, I am wondering why Yoga and Druidry (or is it Western Mysteries more in general?) handle the same 'stuff' in what appear to be opposite ways!

Thank you.
-Gigoachef

replies.title: Re: Four-fold breathing: FILO or FIFO
replies.content:
Different practices use different methods. Prana and nwyfre are the same thing, as far as I can tell, and they're also the same thing as qi -- but you'll find breathing exercises in Taoism that contradict both the yogic and the Druidical methods.

There are many different ways of working with the vital energy (prana, nwyfre, qi, voor, etc.) using breath, and they get different results. Subtle details like the one you've noted here can have significant differences in the effect that the exercise gets. Thus the basic rule is to follow the instructions of the system you're learning!

comments.title: This week's photo.
comments.content:
Are the works of Ross Nichols and Lewis Spence out of copyright now? So if I could find them on the internet could I download them morally.

Also on Karma. Is there any way to gauge how much karma you have backed up and find the ways to pay it back in a controlled and deliberate manner.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: This week's photo.
replies.content:
If they are you then you must get a copy of the original book. Later revisions have a new copyright on the typeset and possible new material, like new prefaces.

I think the UK law states 75 years after the author's death; you should check it first.

And there's also a possibility that D*sney will extend copyrights forever... The asterisk is to avoid using registered trademarks.

replies.title: Re: This week's photo.
replies.content:
The works of Lewis Spence are out of copyright at this point in the United States and Great Britain, and yes, you can download them; here's a good collection of them. The works of Ross Nichols are still in copyright, so you'll have to buy them.

As for karma, it's a mistake to think of it in quantitative terms, i.e., "how much." Karma consists of the consequences of your actions -- the Sanskrit word karma literally means "action" -- and since every action you have ever done in this and every other life has had consequences, all that's part of your karma. The issue is purely whether those consequences are messing you over, and if so, how. The best way to figure that out? It's called "living." Pay attention to the consequences of your actions, and act in ways that create the kind of consequences you want, and karma's your friend.

comments.title: English Cousin
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Thanks you for the advice on illness.

Sorry to bring this up again but I had another visit from the cousin last night. This hasn't happened in ages. I thought I had banished him by including banishing my attraction to him in my SOP banishing.

I have finished DF's Psychic Self-Defense and now understand what the consecrated knife is that the other mage mentioned and have some idea how to go about cutting the cord which I believe must still be in place. I did an Ogham reading, this morning, to see if I should proceed and was told to wait for outside help. Do you have any advice to offer?

I once saw my cousin in a scry and he was on horseback hunting with a bow. He turned and smiled at me and I could see he had canine teeth like a wolf. I felt repulsion but am normally very attracted and energised by him. Some sort of elemental? In the same meditation I was also shown a beautiful pond with a shining surface. My next view was of the same pond without the reflection and it was full of scary shapes of drowned trees. I think I know what that means and that I should have nothing further to do with him. What sort of creature do you think fits this description?

Also, having read DF's book, I suspect that psychopaths are magical creatures, possibly vampires but must study more to have an informed opinion. I have asked our library to purchase your book on Monsters but wondered if you would care to venture an opinion on this hypothesis?
Yours sincerely, Maxine Rogers

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content: Not JMG, but: do you already know about Eurasian steppe people (in part ancestors to modern Europeans, if mostly the Eastern) having looooong been wolf worshippers besides practicing mounted archery?

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content:
Hi,
Normally a mounted archer hunting rates very high on my cool scale but this was not a happy vision. Also wolves are very powerful, beautiful and necessary creatures and I do love my cousin but seeing him smile at me and show his fangs was just frightening.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content: It may take a while to finish breaking the connection between you; keep up the SOP, and keep on distancing yourself from him. As for your visions, those are best understood through meditation, because they inevitably contain personal symbolism specific to the person who does the scrying.

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content:
Thank you for the advice which I will follow. Do you think this might be a good time to start putting a saucer of vinegar in the bedroom?
Maxine

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content: It's worth a try. You can also use half a cut onion, which has similar effects.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Jmg,

Im doing my daily practices with increasing comitment and succees, but i have currently no material time available to add scrying or to continue to the more advanced practices.

My life has an increasing feeling of clearing away old rubble, bad habits, and realising unconfortable things about me. Also, of course, the other side of starting to see exsistence with other eyes. But what i mean is:
It's okay to stay at this level for a while, since it seems than an architecht far wiser than myself is arranging my life just now?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Of course. You can always settle down at a given level of practice and just let it work for a while.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A few days ago, I was meditating outside when I felt a fly land on me. I noticed that when the fly left there was still a lingering sensation in that same spot and an urge to brush it away. Of course I've had that happen to me many times before without giving it much thought, but this time around I noticed a similarity between this lingering sensation and certain "etheric movements" I notice sometimes during meditation. I also drew a connection with a similar phenomenon: as someone who's touchy about his personal space, when I'm touched in any way I don't approve of I get a similar urge to wipe off the spot where I was touched, even if it's just my clothes.

Am I correct in thinking these feelings are the result of an etheric trace left behind by flies and other people?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Interesting. Yes, it might be etheric in nature, though it also might be psychological.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm asking on behalf of an online acquaintance. Earlier this year, a cell phone tower was put up in an adjacent lot to his house. Since then, he's experienced discomfort and mental distress that he attributes to the electromagnetic radiation.

(I don't regard this as a medical question, because no health effects of this type and level of emissions are acknowledged by the medical establishment. Whether this is because of inadequate research, biased or corrupt research, confounding factors, or the actual absence of any such effect is a question I can't judge, and hopefully is irrelevant in this context. I and others have already advised him to seek professional medical advice and treatment for the physical symptoms he's described, and I'm not going to report any of those symptoms here. That said, if this question oversteps nonetheless, my apologies.)

What I'm wondering about is the possibility of cell tower radiation affecting people indirectly by disrupting subtle energies. Because such non-ionizing radiation is essentially extravisual "colors" of light, the possibility of etheric effects in particular seems plausible to me.

Is there a magical solution for protection from direct or indirect effects of manmade electromagnetic fields? The person in question is a middle aged South African man. He practices an idiosyncratic eclectic spirituality that includes tarot reading as well as Christian and Buddhist elements, and he's not adverse to occult practices. It doesn't appear that he is versed in any complete established magic system. But given the stakes (his home and possibly, because his wife doesn't want to move, his marriage), he appears to be willing to take one up if it would lead to a solution.

I was wondering, for instance, whether the SoP would be effective. (Certain aspects of its design make me suspect so.) Though, I'm sure he'd be grateful if something more turn-key, like a hoodoo spell or placing the right crystals in the right places, would work for him instead.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. He might consider talking to someone professional in his area who knows the locally effective magical practices and can advise him.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you, JMG. I'll pass that along.

It makes sense that a wider range of helpful advice could be available to him locally, if his problem is novel or unusual and there's no specific established remedy.

comments.title: Symptoms of Broken Oath?
comments.content:
Several months ago, my wife and I were both on business trips in different geographical locations. Oddly, on the second half of my trip I was propositioned twice, in different cities, and not by women who expect to be paid for companionship. I turned them both down. I am pushing 60, not very attractive and not in great physical condition. I don't look for propositions, and no one has propositioned me in over 20 years. My wife is in about the same condition as me, and we are doing well in our marriage.
I have heard that, when one partner breaks the marriage vow, the other one often gets propositions, even if s/he doesn't know about the partner cheating.
Is there any magical working that would tell me if I need to look into this further?
My wife and her sister seemed mystified (and amused!) when I told them about it.

replies.title: Re: Symptoms of Broken Oath?
replies.content: That's not something I've ever heard, and I certainly don't recommend placing your wife under undue suspicion just as a result of a piece of urban folklore like that. There may simply have been an aspect between a transiting planet and your natal Venus, or the ruler of your eighth house, during the second half of the trip.

comments.title: Questions regarding AODA and Adapting Aspects of CGD
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have read many of your books in order to determine which discipline might be best to commit to. I ultimately believe the AODA is best for me, particularly on account of the ability to adapt certain aspects (deities invokes, etc.) to ones own personal spiritual commitments and devotional practices. I had a few questions:

1. Regarding The Druidry Handbook and The Druid Magic handbook, in addition to working through the Druidry Handbook, how would you suggest working through the DMH during the course of the AODA candidate year? I intended to do my exploration as a Druid to become a Druid apprentice. Are there any other particular books you would suggest to read during the candidate year? I am particularly interested in getting a good grasp of ceremonial magic that can be utilized in a Druid context (or can be adapted). I plan to explore bard and ovate knowledge as well, but plan to emphasize occult practices of the Druid stream

2. As a solitary practitioner, self-initiation will be the way. Not yet knowing what the initiation ritual is like, I wanted to ask if you think it might be appropriate to use a similar anointing, as you describe in the Celtic Golden Dawn, with vervain, as part of self-initiation, followed by divining additional herbs to use in later initiations? It would seem to me that such a process would not be fundamentally contrary to the system of magic used by AODA, but I wanted to hear your thoughts on that.

3. Related to the question above, in the Celtic Golden Dawn you describe a ritual to consecrate a serpent’s egg after going through the spagyric process of collecting salts from the herbs used in previous initiations. Again, not knowing the initiatory rituals used by AODA, would adapting that ritual consecration to fit the system of DMH be an appropriate thing as one moves through the grades?

I realize that some of these questions may be treading the ground of inner grove teachings restricted to initiates, but I thought I’d ask anyway. Any thoughts you may find helpful and are allowed to share, I would appreciate it!

Blessings to you,
David

replies.title: Re: Questions regarding AODA and Adapting Aspects of CGD
replies.content:
If you're going to do the AODA approach, do the AODA approach, without adding or subtracting anything. Otherwise you may well end up like the inexperienced cook who wanted to make a pudding and didn't have a cup of sugar, so used a cup of salt instead! Seriously, this habit of wanting to tinker with rituals you haven't yet practiced and so by definition don't understand is very widespread these days, and very detrimental to success in magic. Follow the instructions to the letter; as W.E. Butler used to put it, "don't monkey with the buzz-saw!"

With regard to the DMH work, it's intended to be done in parallel with other studies in the AODA system. Focus on mastering the Sphere of Protection and its basic applications during the Candidate year, and go on to the rest of the book during your time as an Apprentice. By the time that's done, you'll have another book to work with -- The Dolmen Arch, which will be out sometime next year -- and that will give you plenty to chew on in your Companion years. In the meantime, the reading lists in The Druidry Handbook ought to give you plenty to work with.

replies.title: Re: Questions regarding AODA and Adapting Aspects of CGD
replies.content:
I expected that might be your answer haha. I was thinking it might be compatible on account of the fact that the creation and enchantment of talismans is covered in DMH.

You mentioned in DMH that wands ought to be made of wood. I wondered if you had any thoughts on whether it would be detrimental for one to have an antler handle to a wooden wand? I'm interested in possibly picking up wood working as part of my exploration (working in a job that deals primarily with intangibles, I feel a real need to work with my hands), so the idea of making my own wand during the candidate year is attractive to me. I know many traditions add metals to their wands as well. What are your thoughts on these kinds of augmentations? Any thoughts on what could be beneficial/what to stay away from?

replies.title: Re: Questions regarding AODA and Adapting Aspects of CGD
replies.content:
The Druid wand is specifically made of wood. You can work with metals and other substances in other contexts.

More broadly, remember that the AODA training, if you do it with sufficient perseverance and enthusiasm, will occupy six years. After that, you'll have a sufficiently solid knowledge of the tradition and a sufficiently solid connection to its energies that you can begin tinkering with things. For now, though, follow the instructions exactly as written and you'll get the best results.

comments.title: Dolmen Arch Questions
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I've got several questions about my work with the Dolmen Arch (I've also sent you an email with a general update).

1. As I'm doing the work of enchantment, I'm finding it taking on elements of the scrying exercise described in the DMH. Basically the influences of the cantref I'm imagining in order to breathe them in are taking the form of a physical setting/scene, and are expressing a certain level of autonomy. Is this okay?

2. As I've been doing the SoP with the Inner Grail Working, I've noticed something that I'd like your opinion on. After I do the ritual, especially if it's particularly strong, I feel slightly lightheaded, and have a slight pressure and tingling in my head. I describe it as my head being "filled with light." My question is: what's going on? Am I simply sensing nwyfre? Or is something more specific at play?

3. I've been contacted by at least one entity in my dreams lately. I'm wondering what I can do to follow up on the communication. Obviously I could try praying to the entities, but since they reached out to me in dreams, I'm curious about continuing the conversation that way.

4. Lastly (and this is where I feel like I'm losing it), early on in this grade the only chance I had to do the SoP/Inner Grail Working was just before work, and I didn't have much time. So I rushed through the ritual and actually found it to be more powerful and affecting than usual, and felt like I needed to "give away" some of the energy accumulated by the ritual. So far so good. Then I went into work and started wiping off tables that had been rained on. The rain was cold, and the rag I was using started out dry. Part way through the task, I switched the soaking wet rag from one hand to the other, and noticed it was remarkably warm, almost hot. I handed it to a coworker, and they confirmed it. Is it possible that I heated up the rag with nwyfre? If so, what, how, ???

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Questions
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's common, and it's not merely okay -- it's actively helpful.

2) Good. That's a sign that the highest of the three cauldrons is waking up. You should expect similar sensations in the areas of the other two cauldrons as well, though it may take a while.

3) I have zero talent for dreamwork and so don't really have any advice to give. In your place I'd use scrying instead -- go to an inner place and ask the entity to drop by for a conversation. See what happens.

4) No, you're not losing it -- you're getting it. Have you ever heard of the Tibetan monks who used to have contests in midwinter, after doing various intensive meditative exercises? They'd gather up a pile of sheets, go to the nearest body of water, knock a hole in the ice, and then dip sheets in the frigid water, wrap the sheets around their naked bodies...and dry them out by means of their magically enhanced inner heat. The winner was the monk who could dry the most sheets before his inner heat started to falter.

I don't recommend trying this at home, and of course it's also not the point of the exercises you're doing. Still, it's a sign that the energy centers in your palms are starting to open.

comments.title: The sacrifice of Lystra--an astrological mystery
comments.content:
Dear JMG and others who may be interested,

This is a question from a friend, an art historian who is investigating a puzzle that sorely needs a medieval astrologer’s input.

The artist Raphael made drafts (called ‘cartoons’) of his artwork for tapestries. Professional tapestry weavers then transferred the cartoon designs to cloth. The way the tapestries were woven meant the design was inverted, so Raphael deliberately painted his cartoons in the inverse of how he wanted them to appear. If a character is supposed to be right handed in the tapestry, Raphael painted them left handed in the cartoon. Text was inverted. Etc.

Now for the puzzle. In Raphael’s cartoon “The Sacrifice at Lystra”, the artist painted part of the zodiac. Specifically Aries, Gemini, and Taurus. But strangely, they are not inverted. They appear instead in the odd out of sequence order I listed them in. Aries first, Gemini in the middle, Taurus last. Some art historians think it is a mistake, but that is very unlikely. The other six tapestries in the series are all inverted properly. They were commissioned by Pope Leo, who was a supporter of astrology and would have noticed the inversion. This art historian is guessing that inverted in the finished tapestry, the astrological signs in their odd order (inverted they would be Taurus, Gemini, Aries) would have meant something.

Anyone have any guesses, or know an expert in this area?

Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: The sacrifice of Lystra--an astrological mystery
replies.content: Okay, I'd tend to look for planetary and mythological symbolism here. Aries is ruled by Mars, Taurus by Venus, and Gemini by Mercury. I'm not familiar with the story of the sacrifice at Lystra; are these three planetary gods involved in it? Or is there some other reason why having love and war separated by wisdom, or however else you want to frame the symbolism, would be appropriate for this tapestry?

replies.title: Re: The sacrifice of Lystra--an astrological mystery
replies.content:
Oooooohhhhh, that will intrigue the art historian, I'm sure. Thank you.

Pope Leo was a Sagittarius, but was very close with his brother Giuliano, and they were often referred to as the heavenly twins. It's possible Gemini is representing them as a moderating force among the Medicis.

I've made inquiries elsewhere and will let you know if any further enlightenment pops up.

replies.title: Re: The sacrifice of Lystra--an astrological mystery
replies.content:
also, Hermes is standing in the background holding his caduceus; again the chirality is correct in the cartoon and wrong in the tapestry. This lends credence to your idea that wisdom is playing a big part here. Perhaps the artist is signaling to Pope Leo that his wisdom is keeping peace in Christendom, or something of that nature...

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: The sacrifice of Lystra--an astrological mystery
replies.content:
One more comment--I just read up on Lystra and found that in the story, the townspeople were confusing Saints Paul and Barnabas with Jupiter and Mercury, for the saints had just cured a lame man. The ox and ram are sacrifices for the gods.

So your advice seems super duper right on. Perhaps my art historian friend will start analyzing more pieces of Renaissance art in terms of planetary symbolism. Thank you again! -- Ms. K

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You've said before that sometimes you think your workings are getting strength from people in the future casting them. If the future can affect the present, can the present affect the past? If so, are there any sources you can recommend on the topic of altering the past?

replies.content: Donald Michael Kraig includes a ritual in his book Modern Magick that is designed to let you go back into your past and encourage your younger self to pursue occultism. That might be a starting point for what you have in mind.

comments.title: sweetening or money spell or both for job hunting?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you very much for Magic Mondays. My question refers to job hunting.

A few MMs ago I asked if I could use a sweetening spell to get people to like me more during a networking event for a conference I was going to attend. I did it and left the honey jar near my bed and haven't done anything with it since then (a month ago). I didn't think about using a candle, until I read somebody else using a green candle with the same spell. Now, it's been a while and my husband moved the spell jar this weekend while he was hoovering (vacuuming). My questions are:

1. What is the best way to proceed with a spell for job searching? - i.e., should I do a proper job searching spell - and how, which candle colour/ day of the week/ time and how to go about it please?

2. Is the honey jar spell still ok (or did it lose its power when someone else found it/touched it) and should I light a candle on Fridays at (cannot remember the time/deity) to re-activate it or should I redo it again? If I have to redo it, how do I dispose of the one I have?

3. Any other suggestions on how to or what to do about job searching? - I think I've made a bit of a dog's dinner out of this and I'm a bit confused! Apologies

Many thanks!

P.S. Thank you for the links to that podcast, started listening and it was great to hear your voice... must make time to finish it.

replies.title: Re: sweetening or money spell or both for job hunting?
replies.content:
Okay, at this point you've had some basic experience with magic; if you want to go further, you need to start learning how it's done. I'd encourage you to pick up a copy of my book The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic, read the stuff in front that explains how to design and perform natural magic spells, and then make a spell for your job search your first independent project. You'll find plenty of guidance there.

The sweetening spell should be fine. It won't be disrupted by being moved.

replies.title: Re: sweetening or money spell or both for job hunting?
replies.content:
I hope this isn’t too late. I have always been taught that when you do magic, you focus on the end goal, not the steps before you get there.

A sweetening spell will make your interviewer like you, sure, but that doesn’t mean you’ll get the job. Similarly, focusing too much on successful “job-hunting” might keep you “job hunting” and not reaching the end goal, which I assume is actually working at a job that makes you feel something (happy, relieved, satisfied, whatever your end goal is).

I fully agree that at this point a bit more magic training is important. I also think it’s entirely possible that your spell WAS successful in the very specific thing that you asked for, but was not what you actually wanted. It’s a common mistake in magic, to get the thing you specifically asked for only to find you actually wanted something else. Do magic to be working at the job you desire, and don’t worry for a minute about how you will get there (in magic) then worry about how to find a job only in your non magical day-to-day plan to find a job.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Can elemental pentagram rituals be used on their own, that is, not along with a general banishing or invoking ritual? Could one use them to banish or invoke either the general or some specific qualities of the element in question from either one's own aura or a particular place?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, but they're very blunt instruments when used in that way, and you have the disadvantage that you're not working in a prepared space with conflicting energies cleared away. It's better to do them in context.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I just blundered into founder of The First Church of Dana Scully, Scientist.  I thought it was just a Gillian Anderson/X-Files fan group on Facebook.  Nope.  They claim to be something just a tad more serious than Discordianism or The Church of the Subgenius, a syncretic bunch of Thelemites who incorporate the philosophy of H.P. Lovecraft and revere a fictional character as part of a science fiction cult.  I'd be O.K. with all of it except the Thelemite part based on what you've written about that particular occult path, which makes me apprehensive.  Ever heard of them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Um, no. Thelema's a perfectly workable path if you like that sort of thing; I know some impressive people (cough, cough, Lon Milo Duquette, cough, cough) who work in that tradition. Aleister Crowley was basically a waste of oxygen, but Grady McMurtry, who picked up Crowley's legacy and massively reworked it into a functional system of initiation, was a far more competent mage and also a considerably more successful human being.

That said, the problem with using modern pop culture as the basis for a system of magic is that you end up initiating yourself into the state of consciousness of modern pop culture -- that is to say, the state you were already in, if you're the kind of person who would want to do that. Do what keepeth thou from wilting shall be the loophole in the law, I suppose, but it seems like a waste of effort that could go to something more useful.

Cabbage is the slaw, cabbage that thou wilt.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Am I right to think that discursive meditation does a lot of work on the astral and mental planes?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes.

comments.title: Magic for Court Case?
comments.content:
A few weeks hence my mother has a court date. A couple of years ago she fell and injured herself in a local supermarket’s badly maintained parking lot. She received no compensation, despite serious injury. Apparently the parking lot belongs to a shell corporation or the like in some faraway state, no doubt as a dodge around just such situations as this.

Is there anything I can do magically to increase her chances of success? She is elderly, suffered severe pain and heavy medical costs, and really deserves substantial compensation. I’ll be looking for any relevant astrological elections, but with a month to go the odds are there may not be any. Thanks for any advice you might have to offer.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Magic for Court Case?
replies.content: Any day and hour of Jupiter, now that Jupiter's in his home sign of Sagittarius, would be workable, since Jupiter is the lord of restorative justice and of lavish compensation. Depending on what kind of working you're comfortable doing, candle work, talisman consecration, or an invocation of the god himself would be good choices.

comments.title: etheric(?) plane viewing
comments.content: Hello!  I have been working on opening my third eye, or activating my pineal gland to greater awareness, whichever term seems more well suited.  In the past week I have been able to see what I think is the etheric plane on two separate occasions, for only part of a second, and in my mind's eye.  On one occasion I was seated in my living room, and was delighted to see the walls and objects of the room represented as white energy, comprised seemingly of long strands, and slowly wisping.  On the second occasion, I was in a grocery store. For that same flash of a moment saw everything in the store on the other plane, and was surprised to note that the food products on display on shelves and in cases were that same white energy, but brighter. It made sense - after all, they are energy that we are purchasing to feed ourselves, making literal fuel for energy - it made sense that containers of food were thusly a brighter energy than walls, floors, or furnishings.  These nice moments letting me know I'm actually progressing aside, I'm a bit lost as to how to proceed at present. Generally the things I've read give you the meditation know-how to proceed to this point, and.. what then?  Is there a course of study I should look into, or some reading you'd recommend? Thank you.

replies.title: Re: etheric(?) plane viewing
replies.content: I tend to recommend W.E. Butler's How to Read the Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance to those wanting to develop these particular capacities. Another set of exercises worth considering is Rudolf Steiner's system of training in How to Know Higher Worlds.
